2603.25412.title: Beyond Content Safety: Real-Time Monitoring for Reasoning Vulnerabilities in Large Language Models
2603.25412.paper_summary: Large language models (LLMs) increasingly rely on explicit chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning to solve complex tasks, yet the safety of the reasoning process itself remains largely unaddressed. Existing work on LLM safety focuses on content safety--detecting harmful, biased, or factually incorrect outputs -- and treats the reasoning chain as an opaque intermediate artifact. We identify reasoning safety as an orthogonal and equally critical security dimension: the requirement that a model's reasoning trajectory be logically consistent, computationally efficient, and resistant to adversarial manipulation. We make three contributions. First, we formally define reasoning safety and introduce a nine-category taxonomy of unsafe reasoning behaviors, covering input parsing errors, reasoning execution errors, and process management errors. Second, we conduct a large-scale prevalence study annotating 4111 reasoning chains from both natural reasoning benchmarks and four adversarial attack methods (reasoning hijacking and denial-of-service), confirming that all nine error types occur in practice and that each attack induces a mechanistically interpretable signature. Third, we propose a Reasoning Safety Monitor: an external LLM-based component that runs in parallel with the target model, inspects each reasoning step in real time via a taxonomy-embedded prompt, and dispatches an interrupt signal upon detecting unsafe behavior. Evaluation on a 450-chain static benchmark shows that our monitor achieves up to 84.88\% step-level localization accuracy and 85.37\% error-type classification accuracy, outperforming hallucination detectors and process reward model baselines by substantial margins. These results demonstrate that reasoning-level monitoring is both necessary and practically achievable, and establish reasoning safety as a foundational concern for the secure deployment of large reasoning models.
2603.25412.paper_summary_zh: 大型語言模型（LLMs）越來越依賴明確的思考鏈（CoT）推理來解決複雜任務，但推理過程本身的安全性仍然在很大程度上未被解決。現有的 LLM 安全性研究專注於內容安全——檢測有害、偏見或事實上不正確的輸出——並將推理鏈視為不透明的中介產物。我們將推理安全性確定為一個正交且同樣關鍵的安全維度：要求模型的推理軌跡在邏輯上一致、計算上高效，並且能抵抗對抗性操縱。我們做出三項貢獻。首先，我們正式定義推理安全性，並引入一個涵蓋九類不安全推理行為的分類法，包括輸入解析錯誤、推理執行錯誤和過程管理錯誤。其次，我們進行了一項大規模的流行性研究，對來自自然推理基準和四種對抗攻擊方法（推理劫持和拒絕服務）的 4111 條推理鏈進行標註，確認所有九種錯誤類型在實踐中都會發生，並且每種攻擊都會產生機械可解釋的特徵。第三，我們提出了一個推理安全監控器：一個基於 LLM 的外部組件，與目標模型並行運行，通過嵌入分類法的提示實時檢查每一步推理，並在檢測到不安全行為時發送中斷信號。在 450 條靜態基準上的評估顯示，我們的監控器達到了高達 84.88\% 的步驟級定位準確率和 85.37\% 的錯誤類型分類準確率，顯著超越了幻覺檢測器和過程獎勵模型基準。這些結果表明，推理級監控既是必要的，也是實際可實現的，並將推理安全性確立為大型推理模型安全部署的基礎性關注點。
2603.25412.author: Xunguang Wang et.al.
2603.25412.authors: Xunguang Wang, Yuguang Zhou, Qingyue Wang, Zongjie Li, Ruixuan Huang, Zhenlan Ji, Pingchuan Ma, Shuai Wang
